content:
# Saturation-Height Modelling of Oil and Gas-Capped Reservoirs

**Summary**: The thesis evaluates the use of saturation-height modeling to estimate water saturation and hydrocarbon volumes in oil and gas-capped carbonate reservoirs. It utilizes a multi-step workflow involving core analysis, hydraulic flow unit (HFU) identification via flow zone indicators (FZI), and Leverett J-function normalization to create a robust petrophysical model.

---

## Research Problem

Determining accurate hydrocarbon volumes in carbonate reservoirs where resistivity logs are often limited by shoulder effects and thin bed problems, necessitating a robust method to link petrophysical rock types (or hydraulic flow units) to log data for consistent saturation estimation across both cored and un-cored intervals.

## Objectives

- Establish a geological framework to determine reservoir-wide fluid interfaces.
- Model permeability in non-cored wells using petrophysical rock typing.
- Determine rock-fluid properties and fluid properties from core and log data.
- Define reservoir quality groups (e.g., Hydraulic Flow Units).
- Correct and convert capillary pressure data to reservoir conditions.
- Normalize capillary pressure curves using the J-function approach.
- Establish a relationship between porosity, permeability, and capillary pressure to define rock types.
- Develop saturation height models for different petrophysical rock units.
- Integrate core data with well log data to identify petrophysical rocks in uncored wells.
- Determine free oil levels (FOL) and free water levels (FWL) using pressure elevation analysis.
- Establish a multidisciplinary workflow integrating core data, well logs, and fluid properties.
- Identify hydraulic flow units (HFUs) based on core data interpretation.
- Develop predictive relationships for HFUs using log responses to identify them in non-cored wells.
- Calculate permeability in non-cored wells based on predicted HFUs.
- Establish saturation versus height relationships for each HFU to populate 3D geological models.
- Review saturation height modelling methodologies.
- Demonstrate application on a carbonate reservoir with small oil column and only macro-porosity.
- Demonstrate the use of oil-water and gas-oil interfacial tensions and contact angles.
- Formulate a comprehensive and dynamic modelling layout in a spreadsheet format to handle multiple rock types and capillary pressure datums.
- Explain the physical principles of capillary pressure and its relation to pore radius and interfacial tension.
- Describe the impact of wettability (water-wet, oil-wet, mixed-wet) on fluid distribution in reservoir rocks.
- Detail the conversion of laboratory capillary pressure measurements to reservoir conditions.
- Demonstrate how mercury injection data can be used to calculate pore throat radius distributions.
- Evaluate various mathematical functions for fitting capillary pressure curves.
- Evaluate various capillary pressure fitting functions for different permeability ranges.
- Establish relationships between Thomeer parameters and porosity/permeability.
- Compare the Leverett J-function with other models like Skelt-Harrison and Cuddy methods.
- Develop a workflow to integrate core data, log analysis, and petrophysical rock typing into 3D reservoir models.
- Establish petrophysical rock types based on core and log data.
- Model permeability in uncored intervals using petrophysical rock typing.
- Determine Free Water Level (FWL) from capillary pressure curves when logs are inconclusive.
- Develop accurate saturation-height models to supersede standard shaly sand equations.
- Describe the various methods for determining porosity from core samples and well logs.
- Evaluate different log types (sonic, density, neutron, NMR) for identifying gas and calculating porosity.
- Explain the calculation of water saturation using Archie's equation and related parameters.
- Discuss the impact of lithology and pore structure on permeability and petrophysical rock typing.
- Evaluate various petrophysical models for permeability estimation from porosity.
- Define hydraulic flow units (HFU) using Flow Zone Index (FZI) and RQI analysis.
- Develop a multivariate regression model to predict FZI in uncored wells based on normalized log data.
- Apply these methodologies to a carbonate reservoir with gas-cap and oil-leg structures.
- Establish a robust petrophysical model using hydraulic flow units (HFU) based on core-derived porosity and permeability.
- Develop a predictive model for FZI in un-cored intervals using normalized well log measurements.
- Normalize capillary pressure curves using the Leverett J-function to account for varying rock qualities.
- Calculate water saturation from the saturation-height model and compare it against Archie-derived values.
- Develop a model correlating petrophysical rock types or hydraulic flow units to logs.
- Predict petrophysical rock types in un-cored wells using log data.
- Determine permeability from porosity based on predicted hydraulic flow units.
- Integrate core and log data to produce water saturations based on capillary pressure measured on core.
- Evaluate different laboratory methods for measuring capillary pressure (centrifuge, porous plate).
- Discuss the impact of stress and clay-bound water on capillary pressure data.
- Explain the conversion of lab-measured capillary pressure to reservoir conditions.
- Evaluate the relationship between porosity, permeability, and capillary pressure.
- Develop/apply saturation-height models to determine water saturation and free water level.
- Assess the impact of different reservoir conditions on petrophysical interpretations.

## Methods

The study utilizes a multi-step workflow: 1. Establishing a geological framework using sequence-stratigraphic principles, well logs, core data, and seismic data. 2. Defining a structural framework including top maps and fault identification. 3. Identifying sedimentary facies through core description, log signatures, and thin-section petrography to link them to petrophysical rock types. 4. Determining fluid interfaces (FWL, FOL, OWC, GOC). 5. Modeling permeability in non-cored wells by correlating core data to well logs. 6. Determining rock-fluid properties (IFT and contact angles) and fluid properties (densities). 7. Defining reservoir quality groups based on pore throat radius proxies (sqrt(K)/phi). 8. Normalizing capillary pressure data using the J-function approach.

## Data and Materials

Data includes wireline log suites (Gamma Ray, Density, Neutron, Sonic, Resistivity) for three wells in a carbonate reservoir; core plug data from two wells used for calibration of petrophysical models; and mercury injection capillary pressure (MICP) data to characterize pore systems.

## Key Findings

- Capillary pressure is a function of pore geometry, surface tension, and interfacial tension. ([[2010-alroudhan|AlRoudhan, 2010, p. 17]])
- The Leverett J-function is used to normalize capillary pressure curves by accounting for variations in permeability and porosity. ([[2010-alroudhan|AlRoudhan, 2010, p. 55]])
- Several curve fitting functions are available for capillary pressure data, including Simple Non-Linear, Logarithmic, Simple Exponential, Advanced Exponential, Hyperbolic, Lambda, and Thomeer functions. ([[2010-alroudhan|AlRoudhan, 2010, p. 42]])
- Petrophysical rock types (e.g., Hydraulic Flow Units) are essential for predicting permeability in uncored wells. ([[2010-alroudhan|AlRoudhan, 2010, p. 139]])
- The distinction between Free Water Level (FWL), Initial Oil Water Contact (IOWC), and Producing Oil Water Contact (POWC) is critical for reservoir modeling. ([[2010-alroudhan|AlRoudhan, 2010, p. 33]])
- The gas oil contact (GOC) was located at -4862 feet in the reservoir section. ([[2010-alroudhan|AlRoudhan, 2010, p. 25]])
- The oil water contact (OWC) lies at -4930 feet, with a field-wide average free water level (FWL) of -4937 feet. ([[2010-alroudhan|AlRoudhan, 2010, p. 25]])
- A field-wide average free oil level (FOL) was interpreted at -4867 feet based on pressure elevation analysis. ([[2010-alroudhan|AlRoudhan, 2010, p. 25]])
- Petrophysical rock types were defined using RQI and FZI to group rocks with similar flow characteristics into five distinct units. ([[2010-alroudhan|AlRoudhan, 2010, p. 131]])
- Mercury injection capillary pressure curves for different petrophysical rock types showed distinct clusters, indicating unique pore throat distributions. ([[2010-alroudhan|AlRoudhan, 2010, p. 137]])
- The study identified that vuggy porosity in oomoldic limestone causes a significant deviation from the standard lithology lines on sonic transit time plots. ([[2010-alroudhan|AlRoudhan, 2010, p. 107]])
- The Archie 'm' factor was determined to be 1.9 based on special core analysis. ([[2010-alroudhan|AlRoudhan, 2010, p. 26]])
- Neutron porosity of shale beds was found to range from 0.35 to 0.40. ([[2010-alroudhan|AlRoudhan, 2010, p. 26]])
- The advanced exponential function is used to generate the J-function versus Sw curves, where a non-linear equation for each HFU can replace the Swir term to account for variations in rock quality. ([[2010-alroudhan|AlRoudhan, 2010, p. 30]])
- Saturation-height models (red curve) compared favorably with Archie water saturation (blue curve), showing only minor differences of five to ten units in specific intervals. ([[2010-alroudhan|AlRoudhan, 2010, p. 31]])

## Limitations

Mercury injection data was not available at high pressure to characterize micro-pores; however, since the reservoir had a sufficient buoyancy force to overcome capillary entry pressures of micro-pores, the J-function method remained applicable.

## Recommendations and Future Work

Not stated in source
